item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I’d been imagining that once victims were overworked until exhaustion made them useless for the boat’s purposes, they were liquified as a first step into being rendered down into biodiesel and used as the boat’s fuel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch there, don't know how I missed that. thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to ask — after it takes on a new part, is it still really the same ship?

Jokes aside, I think there's an error in the table of ship parts — the SV Sea Bird's date is after the Toluca's, but the Toluca comes first in the table; I only point this out because the rest of the table is in chronological order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I disliked this so much.

+ show block
– hide block

The current photo is weakening the article, because it looks like a regular ship, not the weird amalgam that is described.

The footnotes are many and most of them are unnecessary, so they annoyed me. "The first of two occasions the door is open", great. The other footnote when the door opens again, duh. The redacted name footnotes, not worth scrolling down.

There's no record of Toluca, but there's a date of disappearance, type of ship, and we know which current parts come from it?

Abductees are distressed for 3-10 days before assimilating, but the D-class takes 2-3 months?

What's so special about the D-class that she has a special, unknown fate? That detracts from the consistent concept of a ship that "consumes and excretes."

The ship doesn't teleport, and is trackable, but somehow snatched a ship in Lake Superior in an additional bit of mystery? That kind of deflates the whole thing. The rest of the article has a very consistent, understandable object, but then it has this tacked on thing just because why? Do we really need an additional twist like this?

item.title: Attribution
item.content:encoded:
Image source and licensing information of SCP XXXX: Original source

Photo was taken by user Wollex

item.title: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
Note: Image source of "SCP XXXX" is still unknown.

  • japanese-vessel.jpg

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public domain

Title: File:120405-G-ZZ999-The sinking of the Japanese fishing vessel Ryou-un Maru.jpg
Author: U.S. Coast Guard photo by Petty Officer 2nd Class Brandon Thomas
Uploaded on: 2012

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's true, but if you look at the dimensions of the st lawrence seaway it has quite a few points along it where it's less than 30 metres wide, and 2467 has a width of 45 metres, meaning it had to get there by other means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
It's interesting to think about an entity that sustains itself indefinitely by picking up new parts and excreting the old.
"Recent observation has shown a hull section identified as having originated on the SS Edmund Fitzgerald, which disappeared on Lake Superior on November 10 1975. Investigation into how SCP-2467 reached the Edmund Fitzgerald despite its location in an inland lake is ongoing." <— SCP-2467 would have traveled down the Saint Lawrence Seaway. That's why so many ships sank in Lake Superior. They would come from the ocean, where the salt made the ships more buoyant, into the fresh water of Lake Superior, where they would sit much lower in the water. Any kind of storm come up and they're in trouble.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Fantastic skip!

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Happy borthsday, and congratulations.

Wow, thank you all for getting my first scp to such a huge milestone, and on my birthday too! I never actually thought it would do this well, maybe petering out around +50 or so. Thank you all so much.

item.title: Re: Addendum 2467-02
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for correcting me on the century mix up. Why didn't they start at 0 centuries!?

My thinking with the cannons is that the original ship the cannons came from was in the possession of a more successful, ostentatious pirate who saw fit to decorate their cannons with engraving and scroll work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "Captain" rapes you until you become liquid, I guess that wasn't your idea, but now I can't take it out of my head. Take your upvote!

item.title: Addendum 2467-02
item.content:encoded:
In regard to the second addendum; how does the foundation know what ship some of the listed components came from? The later ones I can understand as being witnessed by the tailing foundation ship, but the early ones?

I feel like one cannon from the 1800s looks much like any other from a distance.

Only other nitpick is also in addendum 2467-02, the description for Toulca should be 17th century.

Still really enjoyed it. I like scrapwork ship scips.
Ca't believe that my first SCP has reached +50 in votes! This is so great, thank you all so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generally good, but I have a couple of nits to pick:

waves reaching up to and over a peak height of 25 metres

Sorry, this is one of my major pet peeves. Literally every number is either up to or over 25, so this really doesn't tell us anything at all. I would say, "waves typically/frequently/occasionally reaching a peak height of 25 metres," depending on how often you envision this happening. The sentence about outliers already tells us that the waves can reach greater heights.

This is believed to be a form of mind-affecting anomaly, evidenced by any person coming into contact or close proximity with SCP-2467 for an extended period of time (3-10 days depending on the individual) comes to believe the SCP-2467 is correct in its actions, and will attempt to aid it however they are able.

As Buttfranklin said, this is just ungrammatical. It could possibly work if you changed it to, "evidenced by the fact that any person, etc." You'd probably be better off splitting it into two or three sentences, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt that the article stood well enough on its own, and was actually a bit bummed when it bexame apparent that the collapsible was going to be an additional narrative. While it was certainly well-written, it didn't add very much to the article beyond the spooky humming detail, which could easily be articulated in the description.

Still, an enjoyably unnerving read. +1

item.title: Dammit
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I'm really annoyed by the existence of this SCP. Much to my dismay, I had almost the exact same idea (spooky ship that screws over weather and takes people) today. So when I'm looking through keter class SCPs and stumble upon this, I'm rather annoyed someone beat me to the idea.

Regardless, this was an excellent read and very mysteriously. I gave it a +1, then a -1 just so I could give it another +1. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same line of thought here- for me it's also a rare instance of referencing a real-world disaster actually improving a piece, as it raises the question of whether it brings microbursts with it or if it can appear wherever microbursts are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it needed to be there in order to show that this thing doesn't collect ship parts just by physically collecting them, something weirder is happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really strong logs make this stand apart from other ghost ship SCPs. Because, believe me, we have a ton of 'em. Then again, I must like them, because I enjoyed this even before the logs!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but...
– hide block

The reference to Edmund Fitzgerald seems way out of place, and as far as I could tell it doesn't tie in to the rest of the narrative at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just didn't grab me. I've never been big on nautical SCPs, and this one didn't have any parts that really stood out. The log was fairly predictable as well. Shrug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wooo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely fucking fantastic, Balth.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Third.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks heaps for the edits!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changing to Keter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is an excellent attention to detail that makes this incredibly immersive start to finish. Excellent job on the video log! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going to have to agree with Roget. This definitely should be Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded. "No way is this Euclid!" was one of my first thoughts once I got to the description.

edit: More detailed crit after a SPaG comb-through. I like the idea: it's creepy and doesn't feel too derivative of on- or off-wiki material — which is surprising given that, to my mind, this kind of amalgam ghost ship is a centuries-old trope (SS Moloch anyone?). I think this is possibly due to the more modern elements you've included and the addition of the mind-affecting anomaly — either way, well done!

+ show block
– hide block

In terms of the writing, this feels very much like something you pushed out all at once without much proofreading, as evidenced by the number of SPaG corrections I made. (Sorry!) I'm actually quite impressed that you didn't do much checking over, because the story structure is pretty good: you've presented the information in a nice narrative order, and there aren't any sections that feel especially weaker than the others. And shaggydredlocks has taken the words right out of my mouth re: detail. But if you zoom onto the sentence level, some parts come across a bit awkward or breathless:

This is believed to be a form of mind-affecting anomaly, evidenced by any person coming into contact or close proximity with SCP-2467 for an extended period of time (3-10 days depending on the individual) comes to believe the SCP-2467 is correct in its actions, and will attempt to aid it however they are able.

This is just ungrammatical, I'm afraid. I didn't want to edit this because then I'd inevitably be imposing my own style upon it, and this kind of phrasing should be totally up to the author. I'd recommend trying to express this idea more succinctly, or just splitting it up into separate sentences.

In an effort to better understand SCP-2467, particularly effects and any notable events occurring on board the vessel, four D-Class personnel trained in sailing (D-2467-01 - 04) were provided with a small (9 metre) yacht launched from the SCPS Sunderland and given orders to sail north with the SCPS Sunderland tailing them at a distance of 7 kilometres with the intention of them being boarded and abducted by SCP-2467 to observe the actives of SCP-2467 and its effects on those aboard.

My internal voice ran out of air by the end of that! Sprinkle in some commas, at minimum, or break this apart into, like, five sentences. :P There are a couple of other sections with similar problems. (Additionally, that "-01 - 04" is slightly ambiguous — I'd personally go for something like "-01 to -04".) Oh dang, and I just noticed "to observe the actives of".

Foundation satellites are to constantly track the movement of SCP-2467, with analysts and programs predicting the movements of the object, with plants in the meteorological bureau to divert traffic from areas likely to be visited by SCP-2467.

Double "with + noun" there. Repetition of structure or words crops up now and then throughout the piece, and should be rephrased when possible.


Please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Overall verdict: good idea executed with good storytelling ability, which brings me very close to upvoting, but too much of the wording is too clumsy for me to do so right now. Go over this sentence by sentence and patch those minor issues up, and I'll happily +1.

edit 2: one more thing I forgot to mention: the non-D-class folks on the ship seem an integral part of this anomaly, and an interesting one, so you could definitely spend more time talking about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I'll see what the consensus is and then take action from there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I think it should be Keter. The Foundation has no reliable way to contain it other than tracking it and spreading disinformation campaigns to cover up the attacks. It's an active and uncontained threat. I really liked the imagery of a ship cobbled together from other vessels, and the log was nicely spooky. +1
